narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 44.5% chance that you would fail it—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 30.50, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles both at 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment status. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is notably higher at 37.27, and their Year 1 and Year 3 deciles average around 6 and 5.9, respectively, placing your earlier performance in the lower range compared to them. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 decile, Year 1 decile, and undergraduate GPA, in that order of impact. Your Year 3 decile of 2 had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as it signaled weaker relative performance in your school compared to bar passers. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2 contributed toward that same concerning direction, reinforcing early academic challenges. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 stood out as the top factor pulling in the opposite direction—toward predicting success—since it exceeds the 3.24 average among those who passed, showing strength in your prior college work. Your middle family income aligns with about a third of bar passers, and your full-time status matches over 90% of them, so those played smaller roles overall. While your LSAT score is below the typical passer, it wasn't among the top drivers here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your grades in subsequent years to climb those deciles, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources early. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas—your solid UGPA gives you a strong foundation to build on. Reapplying after these improvements could shift the prediction in your favor.
